overview.summary: Two Pointers uses two moving indices to narrow the search space without nested loops.
learningObjectives: Know when opposite-direction pointers are valid.
Explain why sorted order makes pointer moves meaningful.
Trace pointer movement visually before reading code.
overview.interviewRelevance: It is one of the most reused linear-time interview patterns.

paragraphs: Imagine two people scanning a queue from opposite ends to find a pair that meets a rule. They do not need to restart from scratch every time.
Whenever the data is ordered, pointer movement tells you which side can be safely discarded.
realWorldHook.ahaMoment: One comparison can eliminate an entire set of impossible pairs.

definition.technicalDefinition: Two Pointers is a pattern that maintains two indices whose movement is guided by the relationship between the current state and the target condition.

vocabulary.definition: The index tracking the low side of the search.
The index tracking the high side of the search.
Move one pointer because a whole region can no longer contain the answer.

keyProperties: Most useful on sorted arrays or when relative order has meaning.
Often replaces O(n²) pair scanning with O(n).

metaphor: Two people walk toward each other across a bridge, comparing notes at each meeting point.
mentalModel.clickSentence: If the array is sorted, moving one side changes the sum in a predictable direction.

memoryDescription: Two Pointers uses only the original array plus two integer indices.
The pattern is cheap in memory because the state is mostly positional.

steps.explanation: The sum is too small, so move the left pointer right to increase it.
The sum is too small, so move the left pointer right to increase it.
The sum is too large, so move the right pointer left to decrease it.
The sum is too small, so move the left pointer right to increase it.
The sum is too small, so move the left pointer right to increase it.
The pair matches the target, so we can stop here.

operations.name: Compare Pair
operations.description: Inspect the current left/right pair to see how it relates to the target.
operations.visualNote: Both pointer markers stay in view while the compared cells are tinted.

operations.name: Move the Correct Pointer
operations.description: Shift the pointer that can improve the answer while preserving correctness.
operations.visualNote: Only one pointer moves after each comparison.

patternTemplate.pseudocode:
left = 0
right = n - 1
while left < right:
  evaluate(left, right)
  move the pointer that discards impossible answers
patternTemplate.whenToUse: Use Two Pointers when pair relationships matter and order lets you reason about which side to move.
patternTemplate.timeComplexity: O(n) because each pointer crosses the array at most once.

recognitionQuiz.problemStatement: A sorted array contains numbers. Find whether any pair sums to a target value.
recognitionQuiz.explanation: Sorted order lets one comparison tell you which side to move next.

examples.title: Two Sum II
examples.problemStatement: Find two numbers in a sorted array that add up to a target.
steps: Start with the widest possible pair.
If the sum is too large, decrease the right side.
If the sum is too small, increase the left side.
Stop when the pair matches the target.
examples.rationale: Each comparison removes one impossible side.

examples.title: Valid Palindrome
examples.problemStatement: Compare a string from both ends until the middle.
examples.rationale: You only compare matching symmetric positions.

conceptQuiz.question: Why does sorted order matter?
conceptQuiz.explanation: Without sorted order, moving left or right would not predictably change the result.

conceptQuiz.question: What happens if the current sum is too large?
conceptQuiz.explanation: Reducing the larger side decreases the sum on a sorted array.

conceptQuiz.question: Why is the runtime O(n)?
options: Each pointer moves at most n steps

conceptQuiz.question: What does each move do conceptually?
conceptQuiz.explanation: Pointer movement removes regions that cannot contain the answer anymore.

practice.title: Two Sum II
practice.keyInsight: Move one side based on the current sum.

practice.title: Squares of a Sorted Array
practice.keyInsight: Largest square comes from one of the ends.

practice.title: 3Sum
practice.keyInsight: Fix one value and run two pointers inside.

practice.title: 3Sum Closest
practice.keyInsight: Pointer movement continuously improves the candidate.

practice.title: Sort Colors
practice.keyInsight: Same-direction pointers also count as two-pointer thinking.

practice.title: Trapping Rain Water
practice.keyInsight: Move the side with the smaller boundary.
